claim: After a chase, greyhounds fixate significantly on the last known location of the prey object — at straight tracks where the lure stops, fixation odds were 8.34x higher than at racetracks (p=0.009).
stance_target: a galgo that has chased a prey object (or trigger) will return to or remain near the last-known location of the stimulus — directly informing search area prediction
scope_conditions: racing greyhounds in NSW, Australia; N=626 total (89 straight track + 537 racetrack); study period 2017
quoted_support: Fixation on the lure was significantly more likely at the straight tracks (OR = 8.34, z = 2.611, p = 0.009) than at racetracks.

claim: Chase behavior in sighthounds may be maintained by sound cues more than visual cues — racetrack lures emit continuous loud sound that persists after the chase, potentially competing with or confusing the last-known-location fixation.
stance_target: galgo pursuit of prey is sound-driven as well as sight-driven; recovery strategies involving sound (recorded prey calls, familiar dog sounds) may activate approach behavior
quoted_support: Sound of the lure, rather than visual stimuli associated with it, may be the most salient stimulus in this environment... sound is believed by a majority of racing greyhound industry participants in Australia to be more important than visual stimuli.

claim: Sighthound (greyhound) chase behavior is internally motivated (appetitive) — dogs derive reinforcement from performing the chase itself, not only from capturing prey — meaning chase can be sustained even when prey is not visible.
stance_target: a lost galgo that has been triggered into flight/chase mode may sustain movement for extended periods without visible prey, greatly increasing displacement distance
scope_conditions: greyhound breed; racing and training context
quoted_support: Greyhounds may chase lures because of their internal motivation to perform the behaviours involved and they derive direct reinforcement from the performance of those behaviours themselves (appetitive behaviours).

claim: When greyhounds do gain access to a chase object (lure), frustration-related behaviors in the catching pen are reduced — suggesting that consummatory contact (e.g., catching something) terminates the chase-drive arousal state.
stance_target: a galgo that catches prey will de-escalate chase drive and may be more approachable shortly after — behavioral window for recovery may exist post-catch
scope_conditions: racing greyhounds; tracks where lure is accessible vs inaccessible
quoted_support: Handler-assisted rise was significantly less likely to occur at tracks with chase objects accessible in the catching pen (OR = 0.04, z = −3.796, p < 0.001).

claim: Greyhound hare-coursing success rate is approximately 13% — meaning the chase drive is maintained even at very low prey capture success rates, with the chase itself being self-reinforcing.
stance_target: a galgo will persist in chase/flight behavior even without successful prey capture; repeated failed approaches will not extinguish the behavior
scope_conditions: greyhound coursing with live hares in Éire; Reid et al. 2007
quoted_support: Evidence from hare-coursing competitions in Éire suggests that greyhounds capture approximately 13% of hares in that context.
